title: Ditch the niceties in AI prompts to save energy use, say researchers
summary: A UN report warns of the rapid growth in AI energy consumption, but suggests users can improve efficiency by making prompts more concise

title: Ötzi's frozen remains may harbour metabolically active microbes
summary: Researchers studying a 5300-year-old mummified man have identified bacteria that lived in his gut when he was alive, as well as cold-tolerant fungi that colonised his body after death

title: Why you need to future-proof your brain in middle age and how to start
summary: Ages 40 to 65 see a period of turmoil in the brain that has previously been overlooked. But identifying problems during this time can protect your cognitive health for decades to come

title: 'Transformative' pancreatic cancer drug doubles survival time
summary: People with advanced pancreatic cancer taking an experimental daily pill lived nearly twice as long as those receiving chemotherapy infusions

title: A golden age of maths is dawning and mathematicians are freaking out
summary: Mathematicians are stunned at the progress AI is making in solving advanced problems, leaving some questioning whether there will still be room for humans

title: How human error became a weapon against large language models
summary: Alan Turing proposed a test for machine intelligence: could a computer convince a human it was human? We have begun conducting the same test on ourselves, writes Max Moser

title: Geoengineering can thicken Arctic sea ice, but for how long?
summary: Two companies are aiming to preserve Arctic ice by pumping water onto the sheet and letting it freeze, but only one of the trials found that this delayed melting in the summer

title: The best new science-fiction books of June 2026
summary: There is plenty of intriguing sci-fi on offer this month, whether it’s solar-powered cities from Adrian Tchaikovsky or a strange future from M. John Harrison

title: Aim high but don't shoot for the moon, mathematicians advise
summary: According to a mathematical model of how people weigh up different outcomes, the optimal strategy is to be ambitious, but not overly so

title: Pancreatic cancer halted by virus injection in three patients
summary: A cancer-killing virus has stopped pancreatic tumours from growing and spreading in three people in an initial safety trial, raising hopes that it may help to beat the deadly condition

title: Q-Day could destroy bitcoin – and our retirement savings
summary: Even if you’ve never bought any cryptocurrency, like columnist Karmela Padavic-Callaghan, your money may be affected by bitcoin’s fate – which is uncertain, as quantum computing advances are threatening to make the encryption protecting it useless

title: Glaciers in the 'roof of the world' have suddenly started melting
summary: Until recently, the Pamir mountains in central Asia have bucked the global melting trend, but in 2025, the region’s glaciers experienced a massive loss of ice due to extreme heat

title: Mathematical AI helps researchers crack 50-year-old problem
summary: After an AI from OpenAI found a trick to solve an 80-year-old conjecture from Paul Erdős, mathematicians have borrowed the same technique to solve another important problem

title: Start-ups are racing to revolutionise mathematics with AI
summary: AI start-ups with hundreds of millions of dollars in funding are hiring mathematicians and building AI systems that they hope will not only solve mathematics, but also build more intelligent AI

title: 3D-printed lymph nodes could widen access to CAR T-cell therapy
summary: The cost of CAR T-cell therapy means that the highly effective cancer treatment is unavailable in many parts of the world. But a new way of making these cells could dramatically drive down the cost

title: Our verdict on Luminous by Silvia Park: a fascinating take on robots
summary: The New Scientist Book Club read Silvia Park's near-future sci-fi novel Luminous in May, and had lots of good things to say (along with a few complaints)

title: The late Ian Watson's sci-fi The Embedding is intriguing – but dated
summary: Watson's death last month prompted sci-fi columnist Emily H. Wilson to read his acclaimed 1973 debut and find out what she'd been missing. She found it fascinating – but reflective of its time

title: Unsettling dance piece explores how AI is warping human relationships
summary: Inspired by Shannon Vallor's book The AI Mirror, this compelling piece looks at how we are being affected by our deepening interactions with tech

title: Embryos made without sperm or eggs reveal why many pregnancies fail
summary: Embryo organoids made from stem cells are enabling scientists to recreate early pregnancy in the lab, unlocking treatments for infertility, miscarriage and pre-eclampsia
